title: The neighbour's window
summary: A child watches a window across the road every evening for one minute. The window goes dark for a week, and the child finds out why.
body:
Across the road, there is a window with a yellow curtain. In the evening, the curtain is open. A small light comes on. Someone is inside.

I do not know who lives there. I am eight. My mother says it is not polite to look. But I look. I look every evening for one minute.

What I see is very small. A teapot. A hand pouring water. A book on a table. Sometimes a cat walks past the window. Sometimes a door opens and someone — a woman, I think — walks out of the room.

I do not know her name. I do not know her face. I only know the window.

Last week, the window was dark. The curtain was closed. There was no light. I waited every evening. The window stayed dark.

I told my mother. I was almost crying.

My mother went outside. She walked to the house. She knocked at the door. A man opened it. He said, 'My mother is in hospital. She is fine. She will be home next week.'

The next week, the light came on again. The curtain was open. I saw the teapot. I saw the hand. I saw the cat.

I do not know her name. But I know she came back. That is enough for now.

annotations.meaningVi: đang nằm viện (không dùng 'the' trong tiếng Anh Anh)

grammarNotes.title: The definite article 'the'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'the' trước danh từ đã được nhắc đến hoặc đã rõ với cả hai bên. Trong bài, người kể lần đầu nói 'a window' (một cửa sổ); sau đó luôn dùng 'the window', 'the curtain', 'the light', vì người đọc đã biết.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'the' for nouns already mentioned or already understood. The narrator first says 'a window'; after that, the story uses 'the window', 'the curtain', 'the light' — because the reader now knows which one.

cloze.template:
Across the road, there is a window with a {{b1}} curtain. In the {{b2}}, the curtain is open. A small light comes on. Someone is inside.

I do not know who lives there. I am {{b3}}. My mother says it is not {{b4}} to look. But I look. I look every evening for one minute.

What I see is very small. A {{b5}}. A hand pouring water. A book on a table. Sometimes a {{b6}} walks past the window. Sometimes a door opens and someone — a woman, I think — walks out of the room.

I do not know her {{b7}}. I do not know her face. I only know the window.

Last week, the window was {{b8}}. The curtain was {{b9}}. There was no light. I waited every evening. The window stayed dark.

I told my mother. I was almost crying.

My mother went outside. She walked to the house. She {{b10}} at the door. A man opened it. He said, 'My mother is in {{b11}}. She is fine. She will be home next week.'

The next week, the light came on again. The curtain was open. I saw the teapot. I saw the hand. I saw the cat.

I do not know her name. But I know she came back. That is {{b12}} for now.

blanks.explanation: 'In the evening, the curtain is open.'
Her mother says it is not polite to look — looking into a neighbour's home.
A teapot is one of the small things she sees.
'Sometimes a cat walks past the window.'
She does not know the woman's name.
Last week, the window was dark — no light inside.
Her mother knocked at the door.
The man said, 'My mother is in hospital.'
'That is enough for now.' She doesn't need to know more.

translationVi:
Bên kia đường có một ô cửa sổ với một tấm rèm vàng. Buổi tối, tấm rèm được mở. Một ngọn đèn nhỏ bật lên. Có ai đó ở trong.

Em không biết ai sống ở đó. Em tám tuổi. Mẹ nói nhìn như vậy là không lịch sự. Nhưng em vẫn nhìn. Em nhìn mỗi tối một phút.

Những gì em thấy rất nhỏ. Một ấm trà. Một bàn tay đang rót nước. Một cuốn sách trên bàn. Đôi khi một con mèo đi ngang qua cửa sổ. Đôi khi một cánh cửa mở ra và ai đó — em nghĩ là một người phụ nữ — bước ra khỏi phòng.

Em không biết tên bà. Em không biết mặt bà. Em chỉ biết cửa sổ.

Tuần trước, cửa sổ tối. Tấm rèm đóng. Không có ánh đèn. Em đợi mỗi tối. Cửa sổ vẫn tối.

Em kể với mẹ. Em gần như khóc.

Mẹ em đi ra ngoài. Mẹ đi sang nhà bên đó. Mẹ gõ cửa. Một người đàn ông mở cửa. Chú nói: 'Mẹ của tôi đang ở bệnh viện. Bà ấy không sao. Bà ấy sẽ về tuần sau.'

Tuần sau, đèn lại bật lên. Tấm rèm được mở. Em thấy ấm trà. Em thấy bàn tay. Em thấy con mèo.

Em không biết tên bà. Nhưng em biết bà đã về. Bây giờ thế là đủ.

questions.prompt: What colour is the curtain in the window across the road?
What does the writer's mother say about looking into the window?
questions.explanation: 'My mother says it is not polite to look.'

questions.prompt: Which three things does the writer see through the window?
options: A bicycle, a TV, a phone
A teapot, a hand pouring water, a book on a table
A computer, a printer, a desk
A guitar, a piano, a chair
questions.hint: Look at paragraph 3 for small details.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer not know the woman's name?
options: She has never spoken to her
Her mother told her not to
She is too shy to ask
questions.explanation: The writer only watches the window — she has never met the woman.

questions.prompt: What happened to the window last week?
options: A new curtain was put up
It was dark and the curtain was closed for a week
questions.explanation: 'The window was dark. The curtain was closed.'

questions.prompt: What did the writer's mother do when she heard?
options: Walked across, knocked at the door, and asked
questions.explanation: 'She walked to the house. She knocked at the door.'

options: In hospital — but she was fine
questions.explanation: 'My mother is in hospital. She is fine.'
questions.hint: Look at the man's reply at the door.

options: The light came on again, and the writer saw the teapot, the hand, and the cat
questions.explanation: 'The next week, the light came on again.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'That is enough for now'?
options: She does not need to know the woman's name — knowing she came back is enough
She wants to leave the neighbourhood
questions.explanation: She still does not know the woman's name, but the woman is back — and that is enough.
questions.hint: Look at the very last lines.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer says, 'I only know the window.' Think of someone in your life that you see often but do not really know — a shopkeeper, a guard, a neighbour. What would change for you if they suddenly were not there?
